data.title: Q4 2024 Fixed Income Perspectives
data.description:
Introduction

As the Federal Reserve (Fed) embarks on its long-awaited rate-cutting cycle, the U.S. economy continues to display resilience despite mixed signals from global markets. Central banks worldwide are navigating their own paths toward monetary easing, but diverging approaches suggest an uneven road ahead.

It has certainly been an eventful quarter for fixed income. A significant repricing of Fed expectations and the start of the rate-cutting cycle has finally brought the return of positive-sloped yield curves. The combination of positive sloped yield curves and elevated yields has been a rare sight in the past few years, but this is, in fact, a return to more normal times for fixed income and implies that investors should take advantage of more traditional fixed income strategies.

We anticipate the Fed moving from a restrictive to slightly accommodative policy and expect the Fed Funds rate to reach 3.25% by mid-2025. Historically, when the Fed begins to ease monetary policy, it often becomes an important tailwind for fixed income. Given that investors have heavily favored money market funds and short-term maturity products in recent years, the onset of the rate-cutting cycle should encourage savvy investors to extend further out on the yield curve. By reallocating funds into assets with higher yields and greater sensitivity to interest rate changes, they can better position themselves to benefit from the evolving market environment.

Core fixed income, including mortgage-backed securities and investment-grade corporates, stand to perform well in this environment. Although they have some sensitivity to changes in economic conditions, their performance is more driven by interest rate changes-investors should seek to lock in yields before the Fed gets too far into its cutting cycle.

Other, riskier fixed-income segments, such as high yield and emerging market debt, can also deliver positive returns if the U.S. economy can achieve a soft landing. While meaningful spread compression is unlikely from here, the extra yield offered by those lower-quality assets is still a prudent consideration for investors.

Given the current environment of gently slowing economic growth and monetary easing, maintaining exposure to fixed income is prudent for investors. Fixed income assets provide a reliable source of income and yield, offer mitigation against widespread market volatility, and present opportunities to enhance returns within investment portfolios.

Summary of investment implications

Investment Grade Credit

Despite tight valuations, fundamentals and technicals are on sound footing. Although off its high, yield remains a stable factor from a technical standpoint. With the asset class likely to thrive in the lower-but-positive U.S. growth environment, investors should seek to lock in yields now ahead of further policy action.

High Yield Credit

High yield bonds offer compelling income opportunities as relative yields remain attractive, although capital appreciation may be limited. Investors should focus on high-quality issuers with solid fundamentals, given potential volatility in spreads. Strong technical support from inflows should sustain performance, even as deal activity increases.

Securitized Debt

Agency mortgage-backed securities are poised to perform well given their government guarantee of credit risk and tendency to outperform credit sectors during an economic slowdown. With more stable cash flows and lower prepayment risks atypical for the asset class, the sector should be able to weather a substantial decline in interest rates. Investors should consider MBS for portfolio diversification and mitigation against credit risk.

Municipals

As monetary policy shifts, the municipal sector offers investors seeking credit exposure an opportunity to extend duration and lock-in attractive tax-advantaged income. With technical pressures likely to ease following the U.S. election and the near-term path of policy rates lower vs. higher, the fourth quarter may be the right entry point for investors.

Emerging Market Debt

Emerging market (EM) debt stands to benefit from widening growth differentials between emerging and developed markets as global rates fall. Investors should focus on economies with resilient fundamentals, especially in regions where inflation is moderating. EM debt provides a strong diversification opportunity with potential for yield outperformance as U.S. rates decline.

Private Credit

Private credit, particularly in the lower and core middle market, continues to offer attractive relative value compared to public markets. Investors should capitalize on tighter spreads, lower leverage, and favorable credit terms in these segments. Given the resilience of private credit amid economic volatility, it remains a valuable component for income-focused portfolios.

Global bond outlook

U.S. outlook

What a difference a year makes. At the Federal Reserve's Jackson Hole Symposium in August 2023, Fed Chair Jerome Powell spoke about inflation, noting that it had "substantial further ground

to cover to get back to price stability." He also mentioned that labor market rebalancing had "continued over the past year but [remained] incomplete." Fast forwarding to this year's symposium, Powell's tone had noticeably changed; he acknowledged that inflation has fallen significantly, and the Fed's 2% target is within reach, stating that they "do not seek or welcome further cooling in labor market conditions."

With the Fed gaining confidence about inflation as well as concern about further cooling in the labor market, the much-anticipated rate cut has the cycle begun with a 50 basis point (bps) rate cut at the September meeting. The magnitude of the cycle will depend most critically upon the evolution of the labor market. We view current market pricing as consistent with a Fed move toward less restrictive policy and expect the Fed funds rate to reach 3.25% by June of 2025. If the labor market resumes the uptrend that was recently broken (unemployment higher, non-farm payrolls lower), then a more aggressive and accommodative stance will be necessary, and rates would likely fall further.

In fact, we view that path as likely, given how labor market data has recently benefitted from unusually favorable seasonal adjustments relative to prior years and a spike in government hiring, both of which are unlikely to persist.

Assuming our expectation for a soft landing is correct, significant Fed easing is already priced into forward rates. We are forecasting that the 10-year UST yield will be 3.50% by mid-2025, and as a result, are somewhat less enthusiastic about outright duration positions but continue to have strong conviction in yield curve steepener positions. The best opportunity in rates remains at the front end of the curve. However, if the Fed is unable to avoid a recession, there will be additional opportunities in outright duration.

Looking at fixed income spreads, a gradually slowing labor market combined with Fed rate cuts and global central bank accommodation is an environment that should be supportive. Today's spreads are fairly tight compared to historical averages, and potential catalysts such as geopolitical and election risk remain on the horizon. With corporate profit margins healthy, cost-cutting and layoffs are unlikely, meaning that the greatest risk to spreads (recession-related economic challenges) is unlikely.

Market environment

Year-to-date performance, spread, and yield for various fixed income indices

Global outlook

In global markets, moderating inflation has given policymakers and markets a reprieve, opening the door to rate cuts across developed markets. While the Fed was slower to start, waiting until September to begin its rate-cutting cycle, the forward path for the European Central Bank and the Bank of England is a less aggressive pace of monetary easing than that of the Fed.

Outside the U.S., the Bank of Japan has continued its policy tightening cycle, and may continue from here. Despite the move being widely anticipated earlier in the year, the central bank's decision to hike rates in July sparked an unwind in the yen carry trade that had built up significantly over the business cycle, triggering a sharp negative reaction across risk assets. While the impact proved to be short-lived, the move marked a turn in the dollar, as it is consistent with a turn in the monetary policy cycle in the U.S.

What happens to bond yields when major central banks begin an easing cycle? Currently, a soft landing of the global economy remains the consensus view, and despite pricing in aggressive policy cuts, the market projects next year's growth to be at potential. The eventual truth may lie somewhere in the middle, with growth falling below potential as is the norm for late-stage economic cycles.

Notwithstanding, recession is not a necessary condition for bond yields to fall significantly when major economies start to see growth moderate and central banks begin to ease. In fact, in the three previous easing cycles prior to COVID-19, a recession only occurred once, but each easing cycle saw global bond yields initially fall more than 50bps. As the easing cycles continued, rates fell more than 100bps. A similar outcome this time would dominate global fixed income performance beyond the coming elections and the evolution of growth expectations.

Global yields and policy rate cycle

Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index yield vs. % of economies hiking, pausing, or cutting rates

Investment grade credit

Investment grade (IG) credit thrives on moderation, which is precisely the environment that appears to be taking shape on the macro front over the coming months as growth, inflation, and rates are all modulating downward. Despite a more vigorous phase for merger and acquisition activity recently, the moves high-grade companies are making appear manageable for leverage levels, and the market's appetite for IG corporate bonds remains undiminished. With company fundamentals strong and the technical outlook favorable, yields remain attractive from a long-term historical perspective - even after pulling back from recent highs - as the spread as a percentage of yield has risen above 20%.

While clearly not impervious to external factors, the IG market appears to be looking past daily and weekly data with comfort in knowing that Fed rate cuts have started. As such, IG investors have readily absorbed the recently heavy bond supply. While the economy is still growing - albeit slowly - consumers are spending, income growth is solid, and corporate balance sheets remain healthy.

Both the fundamentals and technicals of IG credit are on sound footing. Given the strength of companies' balance sheets, particularly companies with stable-to-improving fundamentals, investor focus should remain on high-grade credit metrics. De-leveraging discipline and favorable funding conditions have companies' credit profiles well positioned. Although off its high, yield remains a stable factor for IG from a technical standpoint.

Despite September's seasonally heavy issuance across IG credit, supply should moderate heading into the fourth quarter, lending positive technical support even if all-in yields are marginally lower. Demand in the U.S. from both retail and institutional investors, as well as foreign demand, has remained strong. Even with rate cuts kicking off, an easing cycle has not halted IG bond buying demand.

Today's tighter spreads in the IG market rightly reflect the strong bid for and the solid standing of IG corporate bonds. However, both the new issue and secondary markets present appealing opportunities to invest in credits with solid fundamentals and good relative value. With IG credit set to thrive in the lower-but-positive U.S. growth environment, investors should seek to lock in yields before the Fed gets too far into its cutting cycle.

Investment grade credit fund flows and yield-to-worst

August 2023-present

High yield credit

Although yields have come down substantially in the high-yield (HY) asset class and have rendered valuations historically expensive, investors will likely continue to harvest an appropriate return for the asset class. Not only does HY stand to benefit from the flow out of money market funds and into risk assets as the Fed's monetary easing cycle gets underway, still constructive credit fundamentals should offset the impact from a slowing economic environment.

Despite facing a slightly more challenging economic landscape, balance sheets of HY companies continue to reflect strong fundamentals. According to JPMorgan, corporate leverage is currently 4.0x, which is still below the long-term average of 4.3x. In addition, interest coverage (a measurement of how easily a company can service its outstanding debt) is currently 4.9x, also well above its long-term average of 4.5x.

Change in maturities since year-end 2022

$Billions

New issuance over the last couple of years has been almost entirely for refinancing purposes, and credit metrics are in very good shape. With the maturity wall pushed years into the future for HY issuers, future defaults should remain well below historical averages even as the economy enters a well-advertised slowdown.

Technicals for the asset class have been a tailwind all year, as high yield has experienced strong inflows but underwhelming new deal activity. As the Fed begins easing policy, deal activity should pick up; while that is welcomed now, it could become a headwind if fund flows reverse.

So far in 2024, the market has already surpassed all of 2023 issuance (which was mainly refinancing, as mentioned). Investors should expect a healthy pace of issuance for the rest of 2024, but the asset class should also benefit from a rotation out of money market funds into higher-yielding assets, including high yield.

With yields heading toward 7%, HY will continue to offer compelling income, but investors should expect that capital appreciation may be more limited. We expect flat to a slight widening of HY spreads, primarily from an expected robust primary calendar, but also from the inability to keep up with an expected Treasury rally. Even with a slight spread widening, however, the asset class could produce higher-than-average total returns, making it an attractive option for investors as rates fall.

Heading into the fourth quarter, the strength of the fundamentals and supportive technical factors should allow investors to produce attractive income in HY, despite a modest widening of spreads.

Securitized debt

One sector uniquely positioned for a labor market slowdown is agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS). Due to the government guarantee of credit risk, agency MBS tends to outperform credit spread sectors in a late-cycle economy or recession.

As interest rates fall, one headwind that agency MBS investors ordinarily face is the convexity effect of a refinancing boom. When borrowers refinance their mortgages due to declining interest rates, there is an increase in principal repayment. Luckily for investors, current prepayment fundamentals are unusually strong for agency MBS.

The average 30-year fixed Fannie Mae borrower currently has a loan below 4%, while today's rate is still above 6%. That large "cushion" between current primary mortgage rates and the average financing rate means that cash flows should remain uncommonly stable even if rates rally substantially from current levels.

Current mortgage rates vs. average existing mortgage rate

2001-present

With banks no longer reducing exposure to the MBS market as they were for most of 2022 and 2023, technicals in MBS are solid and improving. For the first time since 2018, the Bloomberg U.S. MBS Index outperformed the Bloomberg IG Credit Index on an excess return basis for four of the last five months (through September 30, 2024). Since 1990, MBS has often outperformed credit late in economic cycles as the unemployment rate increases. Therefore, while the appetite for credit risk remains strong, the recent MBS outperformance more likely reflects increasing comfort among investors with the interest rate risk inherent in MBS over credit risk. For investors, MBS valuations appear fair both outright and relative to credit, even with recent outperformance.

MBS vs. IG option-adjusted spread and unemployment rate

1990-present

Looking ahead, the MBS market offers an attractive opportunity for investors due to improving investor demand as the Fed cuts interest rates and unemployment increases, an unusually large cushion for refinancing risk, and a lack of credit risk.

Municipals

Heading into the fourth quarter, the municipal bond market stands out as a key destination for investors seeking credit exposure. With the Fed initiating a rate-cutting cycle and valuations appealing, the fourth quarter may present the optimal moment for investors to extend duration in municipal bonds-both for the remainder of the year and potentially throughout the rate cycle.

The fundamentals in munis remain strong year-to-date, with the number of issues being upgraded outpacing the number downgraded by 3.5x. Technical factors for the asset class, however, have not been as supportive. Municipal supply has surged nearly 40% year-to-date, and additional cheapening is likely as substantial net supply through October, combined with election-related uncertainty, continues to pose a headwind. Compared to other high-quality fixed income, these factors have contributed to recent underperformance; 10- and 30-year municipal versus Treasury yield ratios are at their widest levels of 2024.

As the Federal Reserve adjusts its policy during an easing cycle, bond yields typically decline. This drop in yields can create a favorable opportunity for investors to extend duration, locking in attractive tax-advantaged income and potentially benefiting from price

gains. Additionally, municipals have consistently outperformed 3-month T-bills after the start of rate cuts, regardless of whether the economy enters a recession or not. This is a particularly attractive prospect in an environment where the base case is a soft landing, but recession risk is elevated.

Importantly, for tax-sensitive investors seeking to maximize after-tax income or are concerned about the potential for future tax increases, the municipal tax-exemption value is at its highest since 2008, providing additional incentives to explore high-quality municipals.

Given that the technical pressures that have hurt municipals are expected to ease in the fourth quarter following the U.S. election, and that bond yields have historically fallen during easing cycles, 4Q 2024 may be the right entry point for investors to extend duration into munis.

Municipal bond yields as a percentage of Treasury yields

Last twelve months

Emerging market debt

While hopes are high for a soft landing in the U.S., EM economic fundamentals remain in a solid position to weather a broader developed market (DM) slowdown. Overall, EM growth should come in near 4% for 2024, and growth differentials (the difference between real GDP growth for EM vs. that of DM economies) should improve relative to the U.S. heading into 2024 - acknowledging that headwinds from a slowdown in China and broader DMs may endure. While inflation across most emerging markets should moderate, exceptions are likely to persist across Latin America and EMEA (Europe/Middle East/Africa) due to buoyant domestic demand and/or depreciation of local currencies. Although China's introduction of long-awaited stimulus in September should support the equity market and generate optimism for further fiscal action, it is unlikely to reverse the country's structural economic slowdown.

Elevated DM interest rates have proven to be a headwind for EMD for several years, and the sector has witnessed substantial investor outflows as a result. However, with the advent of the Fed's policy pivot, the direction of flows should reverse in the fourth quarter and become more pronounced in 2025. Fortunately, in advance of this expected reversal in demand, lower bond issuance and overall negative net issuance (maturities + interest > new bond issuance) have helped to offset the impact on many EM countries due to lower onshore yields. While supply is expected to increase in the near term, it will likely remain manageable.

In terms of valuations, EM spreads remain near historical tights, with the Bloomberg EM USD Aggregate Index OAS at +270bps*. For the remainder of 2024, spreads are likely to remain rangebound with a bias toward moderate widening but with episodic volatility due to falling U.S. rates and a predictably turbulent election season.

Despite the performance of EM credit being less correlated to growth than EM FX and equity returns historically, flows into EMD have been highly correlated with growth differentials. While today, the growth differential is not at the historical wide of over +300bps*, we view an EM growth differential of >200bps as a technical draw for investment flows away from slowing DMs.

As investors look to the remainder of 2024 and into 2025, the key sectors in the EM debt markets (hard currency and local markets) appear to be well positioned, both fundamentally and technically, and should continue performing.

*as of September 17, 2024. Historical comparisons may be affected by index composition changes.

Real GDP growth

2017-median forecasts through 2025

Global central bank rates

October 2020-present

Private credit

The pace of deal activity in the private equity and private credit markets has been picking up throughout the year, driven by expectations of Fed easing and the resulting stabilization in economic growth.

Provided Fed rate cuts are sufficient to ward off systemic events and prevent a shift back to tighter credit conditions, M&A and leveraged buyout (LBO) activity are likely to continue picking up for the remainder of the year and into 2025. The private direct lending middle market will most likely continue to finance a greater portion of the activity as commercial banks and the public loan market remain less compelling for private equity sponsors and borrowers seeking to grow their businesses.

With the move toward easier credit conditions, credit spreads have tightened. In fact, spreads are considerably tighter over the past year in the public market as well as the upper middle market and large-cap private credit markets that compete directly with the public market. There has been more modest spread tightening in the lower and core direct lending middle market, but that has also realized spread tightening of approximately 75 bps from the widest levels of 2023. The spread movement across markets has improved the relative value of lower and core middle market private credit compared to public and larger private credit opportunities. When also considering other important aspects of relative value, such as lower leverage levels and virtually no payment-in-kind (PIK), meaningful maintenance covenants, relatively attractive original issue discount (OID), and more favorable call protection, the attractive value of lower and select core middle market opportunities is even more noticeable for investors.

Average first-lien sponsored middle market term loan spend

Basis points, 2016-present

With public high yield bond and bank loan markets moving to more aggressive pricing and terms in 2023 (and into 2024), upper middle market participants were forced to compete to maintain share and win new lending opportunities. This has resulted in the less favorable aspects of the public markets spreading into the upper middle market (companies generating $50 million to $100 million EBITDA) and large-cap private credit market, including covenant-lite structures, higher leverage levels with more PIK, aggressive pricing on new deals, and existing loans being repriced to lower spreads. As a result, lower middle market (companies generating $5 million to $15 million EBITDA) and many core middle market opportunities (companies generating $15 million to $50 million EBITDA) continue to represent attractive value.

The first lien senior secured nature of the direct lending middle market, coupled with the attractive risk premiums and orientation to more resilient industries, should contribute to attractive relative value during the coming quarters. If the public markets remain "risk-on," the Fed rate path is likely measured and in conjunction with an economy working into and through a soft landing. If the economic path becomes bumpy, the Fed's rate path will likely accelerate in reaction to public markets experiencing significant volatility and drawdown. In either case, middle market direct lending should provide the diversification and resiliency it has provided in many other uncertain market environments.

Forward-looking sector views

Conclusion

Moving into the final quarter of 2024, the landscape for fixed income has shifted meaningfully, with the Fed initiating its long-awaited rate-cutting cycle and the U.S. economy displaying signs of resilience amid global uncertainty.

While the upcoming U.S. presidential election and potential labor market volatility may introduce some headwinds, easing monetary policy provides a favorable backdrop for fixed income investors. With attractive yields and strong fundamentals across asset classes, we remain optimistic about the potential for further gains, particularly as duration opportunities emerge and global central banks continue to ease their policies.

Original Post

data.title: Main Street Capital: Best Suited To Navigate Changing Interest Rates (Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

The income aspect of investing is one of the most important in my opinion because it can help offset the cost of living increases. Burdened by higher inflation levels, it's always nice to know that your investments have the power to generate a ton of supplemental income as a cushion of safety. Business development companies have treated me very well in this elevated interest rate environment because their portfolio of floating rate debt investments were able to generate higher earnings to support growing distributions. However, the Fed has implemented its first set of interest rate cuts, and now it feels like the golden time for BDCs is over.

Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN) is an outlier in the BDC category because of its strong performance and portfolio strategy that makes it resilient through all interest rate cycles. When I previously covered MAIN back in June, I issued a hold rating due to a large premium to NAV. I even titled that article 'High Quality Comes At A High Premium' and it's always felt scary to add to a holding when it's at a premium. However, I will now be going against the grain here and upgrading my rating to a Buy because continuing to have this limiting outlook has caused me to leave a lot of gains on the table. If I wasn't allowed to own more than one single BDC, I would easily choose MAIN because of its strong portfolio that focuses on diversity, effectively risk management, quality underwriting, monthly distribution, and consistent NAV growth.

A quick total return comparison of MAIN against peers such as Ares Capital (ARCC), Hercules Capital (HTGC), and Capital Southwest (CSWC) shows that MAIN outperformed over the last year. MAIN's total return is now approaching 40% through a combination of its well-supported dividend and some capital appreciation. MAIN sports a dividend yield of 8% and distributions are paid out to shareholders on a monthly basis. This may be attractive for investors looking to add a reliable income stream to their portfolio. However, let's first start off by reviewing the current portfolio strength of MAIN and why I believe it to be one of the best choices to navigate a changing interest rate environment.

Portfolio Strength

MAIN's strength comes from its emphasis on a diverse offering of investments that span across a multitude of different industries. This helps mitigate any sort of concentration risk, as there aren't any specific industries that account for more than 10% of their portfolio. We can see Professional Services accounting for the largest weight of their portfolio at cost at 7%. This is followed by some weight in Machinery and Internet Software companies, accounting for 7% and 6% respectively. What separates MAIN's portfolio from other BDCs is that it is split into different categories of investment types. For instance, MAIN has investments in the following areas:

  • 51% lower-middle market companies
  • 37% private loans
  • 4% middle-market
  • 8% other portfolio investments

Since the lower middle market companies make up the largest slice of the pie, let's first focus on the strength here. Their lower middle market portfolio spans across 83 different portfolio companies with a fair value at $2.4B. These investments have a weighted average yield of 13% and over 99% of these positions are structured as senior secured first lien debt. This helps reduce the level of risk involved sits first lien debt sits at the top of the corporate capital structure. This means that in cases where a portfolio company is going through a bankruptcy and liquidating assets, MAIN's debt would have the highest priority for repayment above other forms of debt. This helps reduce the chances that all invested capital is lost in a bad transaction.

Additionally, this part of the portfolio is unique because it has a majority of focus on fixed rate investments. More specifically, 73% of these investments generate interest income on a fixed rate basis. Most BDCs lean towards floating rate investments because this can help them pull in higher amounts of income during a period of higher interest rates. However, MAIN leaves this focus to the other areas of their portfolio and uses these lower middle market companies as a base of steadiness. Since the debt here is on a fixed rate, it creates a more stable and consistent flow of earnings quarter over quarter. If interest rates were to be cut to zero, this area of the portfolio would still generate a similar amount of income.

As an added bonus, 87% of these investments currently have a term of 5 years. This means that we have a long runway of stability ahead when it comes to income generation in this area of the portfolio. This is exactly why I believe MAIN to be best suited to navigate a changing interest rate environment, which I will circle back to next.

Moving on to the private loan portfolio, this is where MAIN is able to reward shareholders with higher distribution because 97% of their investments here are structured on a floating rate basis. As interest rates rise, so does the amount of interest income MAIN collects from these borrowers. This spans across 92 different investments and has a fair value of $1.7B. Similarly, the private portfolio shares a 99% focus on first lien term debt.

Lastly, their middle market portfolio is a lot smaller, being valued at only $184M and spanning across 19 different investments. 98% of this portfolio is also structured on a first lien term basis and shares a majority of 81% focus on floating rate exposure. Therefore, this combination between a fixed rate portfolio and floating rate portfolios allows MAIN to dominate the space and offers a high level of flexible success in different market environments.

Interest Rate Resilience

When I previously covered MAIN, I issued a hold rating because of the high premium to NAV. Since MAIN operates as a BDC, the price can significantly vary from the actual value of its underlying net assets. For reference, the premium to NAV sat at 62% during my last coverage. An updated look shows us that this has slightly increased to a premium to NAV of nearly 70%! Over the last three years, the price has traded at an average premium to NAV of 56%. So if you are wondering whether or not this premium is justified, I absolutely believe so. Seeking Alpha's Quant system seems to agree, since they currently have a strong buy grade at a 4.69.

Unlike other BDCs with a single floating rate portfolio of debt investments, MAIN's multiple portfolio approach makes it a lot more resilient during these changing interest rate markets. With the exception of the outlier year of 2020, we can see that the price of MAIN has consistently risen through all rate cycles. When interest rates were at near zero levels, MAIN was able to still deliver price growth through consistent sourcing of new commitments. When interest rates sat near their decade high, MAIN was able to still provide a growing NAV and provide a ton of supplemental distributions to shareholders because of the floating rate exposure.

The Fed has already implemented their first interest rate cut, totaling 50 basis points last month. I believe that economic indicators point to a likelihood there are more interest rate cuts on the horizon, and MAIN is one of the best ways to navigate this. For instance, the unemployment rate has steadily increased over the last year and now sits at 4.1%. A higher unemployment rate typically translates to lower household spending as people start to dial their spending back. This is accompanied by an inflation level of 2.4%, which still sits above the Fed's targeted 2% range.

Not to mention, the US Presidential elections are still upcoming, and this may create a market environment of higher volatility and uncertainty as investors await the outcome. The combination of these factors can incentivize the Fed to begin reducing interest rates. Although the floating rate portfolios will take a slight earnings dip as they collect less interest income, MAIN's primary lower middle market portfolio is a steady foundation going forward. Additionally, a lower rate environment may actually serve as a catalyst for price growth if you consider that a lower cost of debt can improve borrowers' balance sheets.

As it becomes more affordable to hold debt on the balance sheet, this may cause an influx of new borrowers to hit the market. Management would be able to capitalize on this as an opportunity to grow their overall portfolio value and offset any negative impact to earnings. A lower rate would also provide the existing borrowers in non-accrual status a bit of relief.

Risk Profile

When it comes to measuring the risk profile for a BDC, I typically reference the non-accrual rate because it's a good indication of a few things. First, it represents the rate of portfolio companies that are underperforming and having trouble keeping up with their required debt maintenance costs. Second, it's a good indication of how tight or loose management's underwriting is when it comes to sourcing new deals. If the non-accrual rate consistently rises, it could be an indication that management's lending criteria may be loose. Lastly, I think that the non-accrual rate shows how sensitive a BDC's portfolio is to a rising interest rate. If non-accruals significantly increase during a period of higher rates, this could be a sign that underlying borrowers have weak balance sheets that cannot support this debt.

As of the last earnings call, the MAIN's non-accrual rate sat at 1.2% of total investment portfolio at fair value and 3.6% of costs. For reference, this is an increase from the time of my last coverage a quarter ago, when non-accruals sat at 0.5% of fair value and 2% of costs. However, this rising non-accrual rate isn't something out of the ordinary, since the majority of BDCs are seeing rising non-accruals at the moment. I don't think that this is anything to worry about and doesn't necessarily indicate any trouble within MAIN's portfolio. The higher rates stay elevated, the number of borrowers that default on loans increase. For reference, here are some relevant non-accrual rates for peers:

  • Hercules Capital: 0.9% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • Ares Capital: 0.7% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • Blue Owl Capital (OBDC): 1.4% non-accrual rate at fair value.

Management confirmed that the rising non-accrual can be attributed to consumer-facing businesses. This comes as no surprise, as many consumer-facing businesses are facing headwinds in trying to navigate a market of higher inflation and interest rates that can put pressure on consumers. This pressure can slow consumer spending and negatively affect earnings for businesses in these sectors. However, MAIN has been able to still excel by providing some healthy financials quarter over quarter.

We ended the second quarter when investments on non-accrual comprising approximately 1.2% of the total investment portfolio at fair value and approximately 3.6% of costs. As David indicated, the new investments on non-accrual for the second quarter largely relate to underperforming companies with significant exposure to consumer end markets. Net asset value or NAV increased by $0.26 per share over the first quarter and $2.11 or 7.6% when compared to a year ago to a record NAV per share of $29.80 at the end of the second quarter. - Jesse Morris, Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer

Financials & Dividend

At the beginning of August, MAIN reported their Q2 earnings and the results were strong. Net investment income came in at $1.07 per share, which beat estimates by $0.07. However, Q2 was over two months ago now, and I want to look forward rather than looking back. Preliminary estimates of NAV per share came in at $30.54 to $0.60 per share. This represents a possible increase to NAV of about 2.5% to 2.7% from the last quarter. NAV growth is the number one indication that a BDC is consistently growing their portfolio and can fully support the distribution while simultaneously retaining a meaningful number of earnings. I believe that MAIN will continue to provide a growing NAV over time because it's clear that management values the importance of continually making new investments to boost valuation.

For instance, MAIN recently completed a $41M investment in a distributor of fiber management within the Canadian telecom industry. This is also accompanied by a $74M investment in Victory Energy Operations, a design and manufacturer of industrial and commercial equipment. These are just some of the deals they closed, as a recent announcement of loan activity also reveals that MAIN originated new commitments totaling $270.3M within their private loan portfolio. This is accompanied by total funded deals already sitting at $309.3M. MAIN should have no problem continuing this trajectory of growth with $30.5M in cash equivalents and approximately $625M in varying credit facilities that can continue to fund new growth.

As of the latest declared monthly dividend of $0.245 per share, the current dividend yield around 8%. In addition to this, they also recently paid a supplemental of $0.30 per share back at the end of September. If we take this monthly payout and multiply it by 3, we get a quarterly payout amount of $0.735 per share. As previously mentioned, net investment income landed at $1.07 per share for the quarter. Not taking the supplemental into consideration, this means that the earnings cover the base distribution by a massive 145.5%! Therefore, MAIN has one of the most secured distributions and there are no concerns about a reduction in dividends as a result of lower interest rates.

Looking back over the last decade alone, MAIN has managed to reward shareholders with distributions on a very consistent basis, no matter what interest rates were. With the exception of 2020, MAIN's track record is essentially as strong as it could be when it comes to reliable distributions. Therefore, if you are an income investor and maintain BDC exposure solely for the dividend, MAIN is a must own. Even though the yield is already so high, it's managed to provide a growth track record that's similar to a more traditional dividend stock. For instance, the dividend has increased at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 5.45% over the last five-year period.

However, it should be noted that the distributions received from MAIN are classified as ordinary dividends. Ordinary dividends have less favorable tax consequences compared to the qualified dividends you'd receive from a more traditional dividend paying stock. Based on your situation, it may be worth it to consider owning MAIN in a tax advantaged account to offset the tax burden here.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that MAIN is a buy despite the high premium to NAV, as I believe it is one of the best ways to navigate a changing interest rate environment while maintaining BDC exposure. The fixed rate portfolio serves as the strong foundation for MAIN that helps it collect a reliable and predictable source of earnings over the long term. The floating rate portfolios are where the boosted earnings would come from if we remain in a float rate environment.

Even if interest rates do continue to get, this could actually serve as a positive catalyst for growth and attract an influx of new borrowers for MAIN to capitalize on. I really like how management has continued to put emphasis on making continuous investments into new portfolio companies a way to fuel growth. The strong track record of performance keeps me confident that MAIN will be able to continue to support its dividend and provide meaningful NAV growth over time.

data.title: ManpowerGroup: Disappointing Q4 Outlook Is In The Price (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My investment rating for ManpowerGroup Inc.'s (NYSE:MAN) shares is downgraded to a Hold, as compared to a Buy previously. MAN's Q4 2024 financial guidance was below expectations, which led to almost a -10% fall in its stock price post-results announcement. On the other hand, ManpowerGroup's valuations have become more attractive following the share price correction, and I expect the company to witness a turnaround in the intermediate term.

MAN's Q2 2024 financial performance and the stock's shareholder yield were in the spotlight for the earlier July 19, 2024, update.

In Line Third Quarter Results Were Overshadowed By Disappointing Q4 Guidance

MAN published an announcement on Thursday, October 17, 2024, morning revealing the company's latest quarterly results and its outlook for the next quarter. ManpowerGroup's key Q3 2024 financial metrics were largely in line with expectations, but the company's Q4 2024 guidance was worse than what the sell-side analysts had anticipated.

ManpowerGroup's top line and normalized EPS decreased by -3.1% YoY and -6.5% YoY to $4,530.2 million and $1.29, respectively in the third quarter of the current year. This represented an improvement from Q2 2024, as MAN's revenue and bottom line had contracted by -6.9% and -17.7%, respectively on YoY terms in the preceding quarter.

On a sequential basis, MAN's revenue rose by +0.2% QoQ for Q3 2024, while the company's non-GAAP adjusted EPS declined by -0.8% QoQ in the latest quarter. In its Q3 2024 results announcement, Manpower Group indicated that there was a "continuation of the challenging environment in North America and Europe" and "good demand in the Latin America and Asia-Pacific region" in Q3 2024.

The company's constant-currency revenue derived from the US market, the Canada market, and the Northern Europe segment decreased by -3% YoY, -11% YoY, and -11% YoY, respectively for the recent quarter. Revenue for MAN's Latin American market and APME segment increased by +23% YoY and +2% YoY, respectively, adjusted for foreign exchange effects in the third quarter of this year. ManpowerGroup's revenue contribution by geographical markets was disclosed in the company's Q3 earnings presentation slides.

MAN's Q3 2024 financial performance was similar to that for Q2 2024 and met the market's expectations to a large extent. The company's most recent third quarter revenue was +1.1% above or slightly better than the consensus estimate, while its Q3 2024 normalized EPS came in -0.4% below or modestly lower than the consensus forecast.

On the flip side, ManpowerGroup's Q4 2024 financial guidance had disappointed the market.

MAN is expecting a revenue decline of -3% YoY and a normalized EPS of $1.03 for the final quarter of 2024 according to the mid-point of its guidance. In contrast, the Wall Street analysts were projecting a top-line contraction of -1.8% YoY and a non-GAAP adjusted EPS of $1.37 as per S&P Capital IQ data. Furthermore, ManpowerGroup's bottom-line guidance implies that the company's normalized EPS will drop by -29.0% YoY and -20.2% YoY, respectively in Q4 2024.

At the company's Q3 2024 analyst call, ManpowerGroup noted that it "will continue to be challenging in North America and Europe", especially Northern Europe that will be impacted by "lower seasonal holiday activity and extended year-end plant closures." As mentioned above, both the Canadian market and the Northern European segment saw their revenue decline by -11% YoY in the third quarter. Therefore, it is reasonable that MAN's overall revenue is expected to decrease by -3% YoY in Q4 2024, which is on par with the -3.1% YoY top-line contraction for Q3 2024.

With regard to Q4 2024 earnings guidance, MAN mentioned at its third quarter analyst briefing that the company's SG&A (Selling General & Administrative) costs should "return to the run rate we saw in previous quarters" prior to Q3 2024. ManpowerGroup's actual normalized EBITA margin was 2.6% for Q3 2024, but the company is guiding for a lower Q4 2024 EBITA margin of 2.0% based on the mid-point of the guidance. MAN had one-offs which boosted its Q3 2024 profit margins, which wouldn't likely be repeated in the fourth quarter.

Also, the company's Q4 2024 earnings might take a significant hit from negative operating leverage, especially for December. In particular, ManpowerGroup thinks that December 2024 revenue will be weak with "extended plant closures" and a suspension of "IT projects" as per its Q4 analyst call commentary. The company's margins for the final month of the year could be hurt by meaningfully lower revenue on a fixed cost base.

In a nutshell, ManpowerGroup's Q4 2024 prospects are unfavorable, and this has overshadowed the company's in-line third quarter results. MAN's negative near-term outlook has led me to lower my rating on the stock to a Hold.

Stock's Valuations Have Become Attractive With Hopes For A Medium-Term Turnaround

MAN is now trading at a reasonably appealing valuation after the post-results stock price correction, and there is a good chance that the company's financial performance will get better in the mid term. As such, I don't think that ManpowerGroup is deserving of a Sell rating.

ManpowerGroup's share price fell by -9.8% to close at October 17, 2024, in response to the company's underwhelming fourth quarter guidance. The market is currently valuing MAN at 11.8 times consensus next twelve months' normalized P/E as per S&P Capital IQ data. As a comparison, the stock's historical 10-year and 15-year average consensus forward P/E ratios are higher at 13.8 times and 16.4 times, respectively. Also, ManpowerGroup's historical normalized EPS CAGR was +14.2% for the FY 2012-2019 (a full seven-year business cycle prior to COVID-19), so the stock is worthy of a 14.2 times P/E multiple assuming a fair PEG (Price-to-Earnings Growth) metric of 1 times.

Negatives regarding the Q4 2024 outlook have been priced into MAN's share price and valuations.

More importantly, there are three reasons supporting my optimistic view of MAN's financial prospects for the intermediate to long term.

Firstly, rate cuts could be a potential recruitment demand recovery driver. The company emphasized at its Q3 2024 earnings briefing that "actions by central banks to lower interest rate to stimulate demand" are likely to translate into "greater certainty and create a more dynamic business environment." Seeking Alpha News reported on October 17 that "the European Central Bank cut its policy rates by 25 basis points" which is "its third cut of the year." Recall that the Northern European segment is one of the weak spots for MAN in Q3 2024, and rate cuts in Europe might be a potential tailwind.

Secondly, ManpowerGroup is taking actions to optimize the company's cost structure. Specifically, MAN noted at the company's recent quarterly earnings call that it has "done some rightsizing" to lower expenses relating to "overhead, the back office, the functions" with expectations of realizing "medium and long-term efficiencies." While recruitment demand and economic conditions are things that MAN has little control over, ManpowerGroup can certainly make itself leaner for the future with its expense optimization measures.

Thirdly, MAN has the potential to reshape its business portfolio to deliver superior financial returns going forward. ManpowerGroup disclosed in its Q3 2024 earnings announcement that it "reached agreement to sell our South Korea business, which will operate as a Manpower Franchise in the future." At its Q3 2024 results briefing, ManpowerGroup explained that it is looking at adopting "franchise models" for certain "lower margin markets" with "a higher risk profile" like South Korea. There is the potential for ManpowerGroup to improve its future financial results by moving to a franchise structure for some of its directly owned operations in specific underperforming markets. Franchised operations could potentially deliver faster sales expansion (better motivation and capabilities of local franchisees) and superior profitability (asset-light franchise fee revenue streams) than directly owned operations.

To sum things up, ManpowerGroup's valuations are appealing, and there is a good likelihood of a turnaround in the mid term.

Conclusion

I have a Hold rating for MAN now. My Neutral view of ManpowerGroup considers both the company's below-expectations fourth quarter outlook and the stock's undemanding valuations.

data.title: Spectris plc: It's A Great Time To Buy
data.description:
At the start of the year, we began covering Spectris plc (OTCPK:SEPJY)(OTCPK:SEPJF), an interesting mid-cap company based in the UK that develops advanced sensing and testing equipment for fundamental research, product development, and production quality control. Since then, shares have underperformed, and the company reported first half of fiscal year 2024 results, and a couple of bolt-on acquisitions.

Given these developments, we thought it was probably a good time to update the thesis, especially given that we think right now is a great time to evaluate the shares. As contrarian investors, we like to buy great companies while they are facing temporary headwinds. Spectris is currently facing temporary headwinds, but the company is also making lemonade out of lemons by doing bolt-on acquisitions at attractive prices, increasing R&D spending, and repurchasing meaningful amounts of its own shares.

Most of the company's end-markets saw like-for-like sales decline in the first half of the year, with the exception being Electronics & Semiconductors (SOXX), which is a sector that continues seeing expansion. The biggest decline was in the Life Sciences and Pharmaceutical (XLV) end-market, with a decline of 22%, as demand for medical research remains soft.

While it can be disappointing to see sales decline, this comes after a few years of strong growth, and we are convinced these are temporary headwinds as the company remains well aligned with some important megatrends. This includes the electrification and decarbonization of the economy, a higher focus on pollution control and protecting the environment, and increasingly advanced manufacturing requiring high-precision and testing controls. Spectris is well positioned to gain market share with some of the best-in-class instrumentation and proprietary software that targets specific applications. Its products and services are also well protected by IP, trade secrets, and deep expertise.

First Half of FY2024 Financial Results

The softer end-markets are clearly reflected in the financial results for the first half of the year. To be fair, the company had delivered three years of double-digit growth and the business has some embedded cyclicality. Further complicating things, their migration to a new ERP system that caused temporary disruptions and meant that about £22 million of sales and approximately £15 million in operating profit got pushed to the second half.

The company's gross profit declined by £53.2 million year-over-year, as a result of the lower revenue and lower absorption of fixed costs. This was partially mitigated by cost reductions made by the company, as it reduced overhead by £19.5 million. Adjusted operating profit for the first half of the year was £61.1 million, roughly 40% below the same period in 2023. On the positive side, the company reduced the number of outstanding shares by close to 3.7 million, and it is not done with its current repurchase plan.

Q3 Preview

Spectris reports third quarter results on October 31st. Normally, they share fewer details compared to end-of-year and half-year periods. That said, we expect to hear that some of the headwinds in the first half have persisted through the third quarter, including weaker demand in China and Europe, and a significant reduction in the battery development end-market as a result of reduced electric vehicle (EV) sales. Demand from the life sciences sector is more of a wild card, but overall, we expect demand to continue its bottoming out process. The semiconductor end-market also remains a wild card, as companies like ASML (ASML) have experienced lower order intake, but chip demand continues to grow.

Our base case is for the company to reiterate that it continues to expect its adjusted operating profit for the full-year to be in line with consensus market expectations, in the range of £218.5 million to £231.5 million, with an average of £225.1 million. We also expect the company to confirm that its SciAps and Micromeritics acquisitions have closed.

Growth

The company has a good track record of growing like-for-like sales even if this growth has significant cyclicality, and at times goes negative. One reason for this is that its customers tend to purchase significantly more equipment from the company when they are in expansion mode, as such, Spectris' revenue growth is highly correlated to manufacturing capital expenditures.

When interest rates were low in the post pandemic period, manufacturing capex grew significantly, but it went negative in 2024, and only recently did it turn slightly positive again. Spectris has also been significantly affected by weakness in the Chinese economy, which represents around 17% of its sales.

Research and Development

In any case, the company continues developing new exciting products to increase its market share and be well positioned during the recovery. Spectris increased R&D spending to almost 9% of sales in order to invest in its future growth.

Some new products include the Revontium, a compact spectrometer that can detect up to 6 million photons per second, Zetasizer a robotic solution that automatically processes samples to make round the clock research possible, and the Mastersizer 3000+ which uses AI to provide reliable results for even the most challenging samples.

According to management, its research and development spending has significantly contributed to market share gains. During the earnings call, an analyst asked if they expect to now remain above 8% of revenues, and in which areas they see opportunities to gain further market share. CEO Andrew Heath replied that they do expect to progressively increase investments in R&D and will likely remain above 8% of sales.

[...], we've stepped up investment in R&D, as I said so in my brief opening remarks, I mean that's really as a consequence of the strategic initiatives that we've been running over the last two years focusing on sort of core parts of our end markets where we see we have the biggest opportunity for structural growth, whether that be in sort of drug development, life science applications, whether that's been really helping to support the capacity build-out in semicon all the way through to smart manufacturing, using our sensors, high-precision sensors to enable our customers to make smarter devices.

So we -- going forward, to your point, we certainly anticipate sort of increasing our range. I mean, historically, we've sort of been at sort of 6% to 7% guidance in terms of R&D. We've clearly gone through that. But I think that's consistent with what we have been saying is we've been looking forward to increase progressively our R&D spend.

Acquisitions

One area where we are particularly excited about are the recent bolt-on acquisitions of high-growth quality businesses. We appreciate when management goes looking for opportunities during a downturn, and not at the peak of the cycle. This helps get more reasonable valuations, and get more of the benefit from synergies.

One of the announced acquisitions is SciAps, one of the leaders in handheld elemental analysis. This is a relatively small business with only about $70 million in revenue, but which has been growing revenue at close to a 46% CAGR over the past decade. Spectris estimates the purchase price to be at an EV/EBITDA multiple of ~14x after synergies, and expects the acquisition to be accretive to earnings in the first year of ownership. Despite being a relatively small company, SciAps has a good diversification of end-markets and geographies.

Another recently announced acquisition is Micromeritics, which according to the company will create the global leader in particle characterization after it is combined with Spectris' Malvern Panalytical business. It is expected that the combination will enable strong opportunities for cross-selling. Micromeritics also boasts a good revenue growth track record, with a revenue CAGR of 17% in the last few years. It is also expected to be accretive to earnings per share in the first year, and similarly an EV/EBITDA multiple of less than 14x after accounting for expected run rate cost synergies. It is also worth mentioning that the company has high exposure to clean-tech end-markets.

Shareholder Returns

While the current 3.2% dividend yield does not look particularly exciting, Spectris has a long track record of growing it. In fact, it is a dividend aristocrat with more than 34 years of dividend growth. Where things get really interesting is when looking at the shareholder yield, which includes buybacks, which puts shareholder returns in the high single digits. Spectris is currently completing a £150 million share buyback program.

We like to check that buybacks are not just compensating for stock-based compensation (SBC), and with Spectris we can see that shares outstanding have been declining for several years now.

Outlook

While the soft market environment is likely to persist for some time, the medium to long-term outlook remains very positive. Management has guided for over-the-cycle revenue growth in the 6% to 7% range, and to eventually reach operating margins above 20%. Despite the spending on buybacks and the bolt-on acquisitions, the company expects the balance sheet to remain in excellent shape, and is guiding for net debt to finish the year with leverage expected to be at ~1.5x EBITDA.

Valuation

Spectris is currently trading at a very low valuation multiple, especially considering the quality of the business. It operates in an attractive industry as well, where technical differentiation is usually more important than price, the equipment tends to be well protected from an IP standpoint, and can even generate high-margin services revenue from maintenance and software licenses.

As a result, many companies in the industry trade with very high valuation multiples, including Agilent Technologies (A), Waters Corp. (WAT), and Danaher (DHR). While these companies are bigger and more stable, we find the premium to be excessive.

Risks

The biggest risk we see with an investment in Spectris right now is the potential for a recession in the U.S. and Europe if central banks fail to engineer a "soft landing". This risk is mitigated by technological, end-market, and geographic diversification, as well as a very strong balance sheet and high profit margins.

Conclusion

Spectris is currently impacted by short-term demand headwinds, but we remain optimistic about the company's long-term prospects. We like that the company is buying back shares aggressively while the valuation is low, while going out and acquiring attractive businesses like SciAps and Micromeritics.

It is rare for such a high-quality business operating in an attractive industry to trade at such a low valuation. While it might require patience, we believe investors buying at current prices will eventually be rewarded. The company is set to benefit from several trends, including advanced manufacturing, growth in the semiconductor sector, and increased spending on the development of new medicines.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
